title: Increase Claims
desc: When and how to file for a higher rating -- with DeLuca factors, reduction protections, and strategic timing

sections.heading: :fire: When to File for an Increase
sections.body:
Your condition got worse. You deserve a higher rating. But **timing matters** -- file smart, not just fast.

**Signs it's time to file:**
- New symptoms have appeared
- Existing symptoms are **more frequent or severe**
- You're on **new or stronger medications**
- You need **additional or different treatment**
- Your condition impacts **daily activities and work** more than before
- Your **range of motion has decreased**
- You've had new **hospitalizations or emergency visits**

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 3.400(o)(2)`, if it's factually ascertainable that an increase occurred **within one year before you file**, VA can make the effective date retroactive to the date of the increase. This means you can get back pay from when the worsening actually happened.

> [!check] Don't sit on a worsening condition. File promptly when symptoms increase -- every month you wait is a month of higher compensation you might lose.

sections.heading: :book: Know Your Rating Criteria First
sections.body:
Before you file anything, **look up what the next higher rating actually requires**. This is the single most important prep step.

Find your diagnostic code in `38 CFR Part 4` and study the criteria. You need to know the exact threshold you're aiming for.

**Example -- Lumbar Spine (DC 5237/5242):**

| Rating | Forward Flexion Requirement |
|--------|---------------------------|
| 10% | Greater than 60 degrees |
| 20% | Greater than 30 but not greater than 60 degrees |
| 40% | 30 degrees or less |
| 50% | Unfavorable ankylosis |

> [!quote] If you're at 10% and your flexion has dropped to 55 degrees, you may qualify for 20%. Knowing this BEFORE you file tells you exactly what to document.

**Example -- PTSD (DC 9411):**

| Rating | Impairment Level |
|--------|------------------|
| 30% | Occasional decrease in work efficiency |
| 50% | Reduced reliability and productivity |
| 70% | Deficiencies in **most areas** (work, family, judgment, thinking, mood) |
| 100% | **Total** occupational and social impairment |

> [!tip] Print out the rating criteria for your diagnostic code and bring it to your doctor. Ask them to document findings that specifically address the criteria for the next higher level. Doctors don't know VA rating language unless you show them.

sections.heading: :tools: Building Your Evidence
sections.body:
Don't file naked. **Build your paper trail before submitting** the claim.

**Your evidence checklist:**

1. **Current treatment records** from VA or private providers noting increased severity
2. **Physician statement** describing how the condition has declined since the last rating
3. **Personal statement** documenting functional limitations -- what you can no longer do
4. **Buddy statements** from family or coworkers describing changes they've observed

> [!tip] The strongest increase claims pair **recent medical evidence showing worsening** with **lay statements describing real-world functional impact**. VA raters look at both the clinical findings and how the condition affects your ability to work and live.

> [!warning] Don't assume your VA treatment records tell the whole story. If you only go to the VA when things are really bad, your records may show gaps that make it look like you're doing fine. Get consistent treatment documented.

> [!check] Before filing, gather ALL four evidence types listed above. A claim with just a form and no supporting evidence is a claim that gets a quick denial.

sections.heading: :star: The DeLuca Factors: Pain, Flare-ups, and Functional Loss
sections.body:
For **musculoskeletal conditions**, the DeLuca factors are your secret weapon. Most veterans don't know about them -- and most C&P examiners don't fully capture them.

> [!important] Under `38 CFR 4.40`, **functional loss from pain** is a compensable disability factor. Under `38 CFR 4.45`, VA must consider **weakened movement, excess fatigability, incoordination, and pain on movement**.

`38 CFR 4.59` adds that "actually painful, unstable, or malaligned joints, due to healed injury" are entitled to **at least the minimum compensable rating** for that joint. Pain on both **active and passive motion**, in **weight-bearing and nonweight-bearing**, must be tested.

> [!tip] **Flare-ups are where the money is.** If your condition is worse during flare-ups than what the examiner sees on exam day, the examiner **must estimate the additional functional loss** during flare-ups. If your back flexion is 50 degrees in the office but drops to 20 degrees during weekly flare-ups, that additional loss should be captured.

> [!quote] Example: Veteran's back measures 50 degrees flexion at the exam (10% criteria). But veteran reports weekly flare-ups where flexion drops to approximately 20 degrees. If the examiner properly documents flare-up impact, that's potentially 40% criteria -- a massive difference.

> [!check] At your C&P exam, specifically describe your **worst days** and **flare-up frequency**. Don't let the examiner skip the DeLuca factors.

sections.heading: :search: The C&P Exam for Increases
sections.body:
VA will schedule a new C&P exam to evaluate current severity. This exam can make or break your increase claim.

**What the examiner will do:**
- Compare your current state against the **rating criteria** in your diagnostic code
- Fill out the same **DBQ** used for initial claims
- Document **range of motion**, functional limitations, flare-up frequency/impact
- Record condition-specific factors

> [!warning] The same rules apply as initial claims: **describe your worst days, bring documentation, and don't minimize**. Veterans have a bad habit of toughing it out at the exam. This is not the time for that.

> [!check] If the exam happens on a good day, **tell the examiner explicitly**: "Today is actually one of my better days. On my worst days, which happen X times per week, my symptoms are..." The examiner should document your **typical and worst-day function**, not just what they see in 20 minutes.

> [!tip] Bring a **written summary** of your symptoms, limitations, and flare-up patterns. Hand it to the examiner. C&P exams are rushed -- a written summary ensures nothing gets missed.

sections.heading: :shield: Rating Protections: The 5, 10, and 20-Year Rules
sections.body:
VA can't just yank your rating whenever they want. **Federal regulations protect your existing ratings** under specific conditions.

| Rule | CFR Citation | Protection |
|------|-------------|------------|
| **5-Year Rule** | `38 CFR 3.344(a)-(b)` | Ratings in effect 5+ years can't be reduced unless evidence shows **sustained improvement** -- not just one good exam |
| **10-Year Rule** | `38 CFR 3.957` | Service connection in effect 10+ years **cannot be severed** except on showing of fraud or lack of qualifying service |
| **20-Year Rule** | `38 CFR 3.951(b)` | A rating continuously in effect 20+ years **cannot be reduced** below that level except on showing of fraud |

> [!important] The **5-Year Rule** is the most commonly relevant. VA must review the **entire record** and determine that improvement is reasonably certain to continue under **ordinary life conditions**. A single improved exam is NOT enough.

> [!tip] Know how long your rating has been in effect. If it's been 5+ years, you have **significantly stronger protection** against reduction. This changes your risk calculation when filing for an increase.

sections.heading: :alert: Watch Out for Proposed Reductions
sections.body:
Here's the catch: **filing for an increase opens your entire rating for that condition to review**. If the C&P exam shows improvement, VA can propose a reduction.

> [!danger] This is a real risk. Before filing for an increase, honestly assess whether your condition has improved in ANY area. If it has, weigh the risk of a reduction against the potential gain.

**If VA proposes a reduction, you have rights:**

1. VA must **notify you in writing** with a proposed rating and the evidence supporting it
2. You have **60 days** to submit evidence and argument against the reduction
3. You can **request a hearing** within 30 days
4. The reduction **cannot take effect** for 60 days after the final decision

> [!important] For ratings in effect 5+ years, `38 CFR 3.344(a)` requires VA to show **sustained improvement demonstrated over time** -- not just one favorable exam. If the reduction exam was **less thorough** than the original exam, that alone may be grounds to fight the reduction.

> [!check] If you receive a proposed reduction, **do not ignore it**. You have 60 days to fight -- use every one of them. Get medical evidence, lay statements, and cite the regulatory protections.

sections.heading: :money: Temporary 100% Ratings
sections.body:
Two regulations provide **temporary 100% ratings** that most veterans don't know about. Free money left on the table.

| Regulation | Trigger | What You Get |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `38 CFR 4.29` | Hospitalized **21+ consecutive days** for a service-connected condition | **Temporary 100%** for the duration of hospitalization |
| `38 CFR 4.30` | Surgery/treatment requiring **convalescence** for a service-connected condition | **Temporary 100%** during recovery |

**`38 CFR 4.30` convalescence applies when:**
- Treatment results in **surgical wounds that haven't healed**
- You need **home confinement**
- You **can't use a major joint or extremity**

> [!tip] These temporary ratings are **in addition to** your regular combined rating. They're based on specific dates of hospitalization or surgery, so back pay depends on when you file.

> [!check] Had surgery or a long hospitalization for a service-connected condition? File for a temporary 100% rating NOW. Don't leave this money on the table.

sections.heading: :target: What to Do If VA Proposes a Reduction
sections.body:
You got the dreaded proposed reduction letter. Don't panic -- **act fast and fight smart**.

**Your 60-day battle plan:**

1. **Request your complete C-file** (claims file) to see what evidence VA is relying on
2. **Compare the reduction exam** to the original exam -- is the new one as thorough?
3. **Get a current medical opinion** from your treating physician describing your actual functional limitations
4. **Submit a detailed written response** within 60 days citing specific regulatory protections
5. **Request a predetermination hearing** if appropriate

**In your response, argue these points:**
- Improvement has **not been sustained** (cite `38 CFR 3.344` if rated 5+ years)
- The reduction exam was **less complete** than the exam that established the rating
- The condition is subject to **temporary improvement** and the exam caught a good day
- **Lay evidence** from family, coworkers, or employer shows ongoing severity

> [!warning] If the reduction goes through despite your response, **appeal immediately** -- file a Supplemental Claim or Higher-Level Review within one year. Do not let the one-year deadline pass.

> [!check] The moment you receive a proposed reduction letter, start gathering evidence. You have 60 days -- use a calendar, set reminders, and treat this like a deadline that could cost you thousands.
